1: צוּרִי אַל-תֶּחֱרַשׁ מִמֶּנִּי (ṣûrî ʾal-teḥĕrash mimmenî, "my Rock, do not be deaf to me") — ḥārash: to be deaf or silent. Divine silence experienced as deafness — the unanswered prayer feels like a stone wall. The appeal to ṣûr (rock/cliff) is precisely because rocks do not move; the Psalmist asks the immovable one not to be unmovable toward him. נִמְשַׁלְתִּי עִם-יֹורְדֵי בוֹר (nimshaltî ʿim-yôrĕdê bôr, "I will be like those who go down to the pit") — bôr: a cistern, a pit — by extension the grave and underworld. Divine silence = death, because only the living praise God (Ps 30:9).
2: בְּנָשְׂאִי יָדַי אֶל-דְּבִיר קָדְשֶׁךָ (bĕnāśĕʾî yāday ʾel-dĕbîr qodshekā, "as I lift my hands toward your Most Holy Place") — dĕbîr: the inner chamber, the Holy of Holies — the back room of the temple (from dābar: that which is behind). The ritual lifting of hands was a standard prayer posture (1 Tim 2:8). Solomon prays that God would hear prayers directed toward this place even from a distance (1 Kgs 8:29-30).
3: הַמְדַבְּרִים שָׁלוֹם עִם-רֵעֵיהֶם (hĕmĕdabbĕrîm shālôm ʿim-rêʿêhem, "who speak peace to their neighbors") — the wicked who use shālôm (peace) as a social greeting while harboring רָעָה בִּלְבָבָם (rāʿāh bilĕbābām, "evil in their hearts"). The heart-speech contrast: social performance of shālôm conceals interior hostility. The two-tongue motif recurs in Ps 12:2-3 and Jas 3:10. The lêb (heart/mind) as the true location of the self, unmasked by God (Ps 7:9).
4: תֶּן-לָהֶם כְּפָעֳלָם (ten-lāhem kĕpoʿŏlām, "Pay them back for what they have done") — pōʿal (deed, work — from pāʿal: to work, to do): a different word from maʿăśeh (artistic work); pōʿal focuses on the act as a moral deed. The imprecation follows the lex talionis logic: the return is proportional to the kĕpoʿŏlām (according to their doing). The prayer for divine judgment is not vengeance-seeking but covenant appeal — God is the judge who must respond consistently to wickedness.
5: כִּי לֹא יָבִינוּ אֶל-פְּעֻלֹּת יְהוָה (kî lōʾ yābînû ʾel-pĕʿullōt YHWH, "Because they take no account of the LORD's works") — bîn: to perceive, discern, pay attention to — the failure is moral inattention to what is visible. Pĕʿullōt YHWH (the LORD's deeds): the deeds of God in history that demand theological interpretation. יֶהֶרְסֵם וְלֹא יִבְנֵם (yehĕrsêm wĕlōʾ yibnêm, "he will pull them down and not rebuild them") — hāras (to demolish) vs bānāh (to build): total and unreversed demolition.
6: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה (bārûk YHWH, "Praise the LORD!") — the abrupt reversal from petition (vv1-5) to praise (vv6-9). כִּי שָׁמַע קוֹל תַּחֲנוּנָי (kî shāmaʿ qôl taḥanûnāy, "for he has heard my cry for mercy") — taḥanûn: supplication, plea for grace (from ḥānan: to be gracious). The perfect tense shāmaʿ (he heard) registers certainty of divine response before it is visibly confirmed — faith that the prayer has been received.
7: יְהוָה עֻזִּי וּמָגִנִּי (YHWH ʿuzzî ûmāginnî, "The LORD is my strength and my shield") — ʿōz (strength/power — same word as Ps 8:2 where it also means "praise") and māgên (the small personal shield). וַיַּעֲלֹז לִבִּי (wayyaʿălōz libbî, "my heart leaps for joy") — ʿālaz: to exult, to leap for joy — a physical, exuberant rejoicing (used of the earth rejoicing, Ps 96:12). The joy is bodily, not merely cognitive.
8: יְהוָה עֹז-לָמוֹ וּמָעוֹז יְשׁוּעוֹת מְשִׁיחוֹ (YHWH ʿōz-lāmô ûmāʿôz yĕshûʿôt mĕshîḥô, "The LORD is the strength of his people; he is the saving fortress of his anointed one") — mĕshîaḥ: his anointed one. The Davidic king is the LORD's māshîaḥ (Messiah), and the protection of the people flows through the anointed. Māʿôz yĕshûʿôt: "fortress of salvations" — the multiple acts of rescue collected under this image. For the NT, the title māshîaḥ makes this the language of the one through whom all divine saving strength reaches the people.
9: וּרְעֵם וְנַשְּׂאֵם עַד-הָעוֹלָם (ûrĕʿêm wĕnassĕʾêm ʿad-hāʿôlām, "be their shepherd and carry them forever") — two parental/pastoral verbs: rāʿāh (to shepherd, to tend) and nāśāʾ (to carry, to bear — as a parent carries a child, Deut 1:31). The combination of shepherding and carrying is the image of the LORD who both leads and physically bears the people. ʿad-hāʿôlām: forever — the eternal scope of the carrying. Jesus as the Good Shepherd who carries the lost sheep home (Luke 15:5) draws on this pastoral register.
